coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induce thigmotactic effect in zebrafish larvae via modulating key genes and metabolites related to synaptic plasticity
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Shun
Zhang, Enming
Tao, Junyu
Zhao, Yunyang
Huo, Wenbo

Bian, Wanping
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) recently gain much attention for its medicinal values in treating diseases such as neurodegeneration and inflammations. However, owing to the high permeability of GQDs across the blood-brain barrier, whether its retention in the central nervous system (CNS) perturbs neurobehaviors remains less reported. In the study, the locomotion of zebrafish larvae (Danio rerio) was fully evaluated when administrated by two GQDs in a concentration gradient, respectively as reduced-GQDs (R-GQDs): 150, 300, 600, 1200, and 2400 g/L, and graphene oxide QDs (GOQDs): 60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 g/L. After exposure, the larvae were kept for locomotion analysis within one week’s depuration. Substantial data showed that the basal locomotor activity of zebrafish larvae was not significantly changed by both two GQDs at low concentrations while weakened greatly with the increase of concentrations, and the total ATP levels of zebrafish larvae were also found to decrease significantly when exposed to the highest concentrations of GQDs. Next, the thigmotactic effect was observed to be remarkably induced in larvae by both two GQDs at any concentrations during exposure, and remained strong in larvae treated by high concentrations of R-GQDs after 7 days’ depuration. To be noted, we found that GQDs affected the synaptic plasticity via downregulating the mRNA levels of NMDA and AMPA receptor family members as well as the total glutamine levels in zebrafish larvae. Together, our study presented robust data underlying the locomotor abnormalities aroused by GQDs in zebrafish larvae and indicated the potential adverse effects of GQDs on synaptic plasticity.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs altered the basal locomotor activity of zebrafish larvae
GQDs induced thigmotaxis of zebrafish larvae
GQDs did not induce oxidative stress but inhibited the production of ATP
GQDs suppressed the mRNA levels of key genes related to synaptic plasticity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface characterization of GQDs. (a) TEM images of R-GQDs and GOQDs, and the insert shows the high resolution of TEM (HRTEM) of GQDs. The scale bar in TEM is 10 nm. (b-c) The nanosize distribution of R-GQDs and GOQDs. (d) FTIR spectra of R-GQDs and GOQDs. (e) The whole XPS spectra profiles of R-GQDs and GOQDs. (f) The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s for R-GQDs and GOQDs. (g) The element composition of R-GQDs and GOQD from XPS spectra. (h) Zeta potential (eV) values of R-GQDs and GOQDs in three solvents including ultrapure water (ddH2O), 1 × PBS, and 1 × DMEM.

Biodistribution and depuration of R-GQDs in zebrafish larvae after exposure and microinjection. (a) Visualization of the distribution of R-GQDs (100 g/L) in zebrafish larvae Tg (fli: EGFP) at 7 dpf under 10 × CLSM. (b) A continuous observation of the retention of R-GQDs (100 g/L) in zebrafish larvae during exposure (6 dpf and 7 dpf) and depuration stage (10 dpf, 14 dpf, and 18 dpf) under the invert fluorescent microscope. (c) An exhibition of zebrafish larvae at 10 dpf, 14 dpf, and 18 dpf (equal to 4 day, 8 day, and 12 days post microinjection, respectively) when micro-injected by 4 nL R-GQDs (1 mg/mL). Yellow arrows indicated the distribution and accumulation of R-GQDs in different organs. (d) Image of the excretion of GQDs in zebrafish larvae at 14 dpf during depuration, the blue arrow denotes the feces.

Basal locomotor activity analysis of zebrafish exposed to GQDs at different concentrations. (a) A simple graphic display of the experimental timeline for locomotion analysis and other research purpose. (b) The record of the moving routine of zebrafish exposed to R-GQDs and GOQDs at 7dpf (n = 24 per group) and the following depuration stage from 10 dpf (n = 24 per group) to 14 dpf (n = 20 per group). (c-d) Statistical results of the total distance moved and average velocity of zebrafish larvae exposed to R-GQDs and GOQDs at 7 dpf and the following depuration process (10 dpf and 14 dpf). Data of control and different treatment groups were compared with One-way ANOVA and the p values of the post-hoc test are * = p ≤ 0.05, * * p ≤ 0.05, and * ** p ≤ 0.001.

A decomposition of the total distance moved of zebrafish larvae during alternating 10-min light and dark periods for a total of 50 min. The whole data profiling of the distance moved per two min of zebrafish larvae exposed to R-GQDs (a) and GOQDs (b) at 7 dpf and the successive depuration stage at 10 dpf and 14 dpf. The total distance travelled of zebrafish larvae during the light and dark period (10 min) were respectively generalized. One-way ANOVA method followed by Dunnett's t test was used to compare the data between control and different treatments. The significant levels are presented as the follows: p ≤ 0.05, * * p ≤ 0.05, and * ** p ≤ 0.001.

Thigmotactic effect of zebrafish larvae induced by GQDs. (a) A portrait of the detection setting of thigmotaxis of zebrafish larvae in 24-well plates. (b-c) Summary of the swimming frequency (%) of zebrafish larvae spending in the outer region when exposed to R-GQDs and GOQDs at 7 dpf and during depuration stage from 10 dpf to 14 dpf. (d-e) Detailed heatmaps of the retention time of zebrafish larvae in the outer region during alternating 10-min light and dark periods for a total of 50 min.

Changes of the ROS levels and total ATP contents in zebrafish exposed to GQDs at 7 dpf. (a) ROS levels in zebrafish larvae during exposure (7 dpf) exposed to different concentrations of R-GQDs (150, 600, and 2400 g/L) and GOQDs (60, 240, and 960 g/L). (b) ATP production in zebrafish larvae during exposure (7 dpf) exposed to different concentrations of R-GQDs (150, 600, and 2400 g/L) and GOQDs (60, 240, and 960 g/L). Each treatment was performed for four biological replicates (n = 30 larvae per replicate) and the comparison between control and different treatments was carried out with One-Way ANOAWA, p ≤ 0.05, * * p ≤ 0.05. (c) Fluorescent images of zebrafish larvae Tg (Na+/K+-ATPase: EGFP) exposed to different concentrations of GQDs at 7 dpf, the yellow arrows indicate the pronephric region.

GQDs downregulated the mRNA levels of synaptic plasticity-related key genes. The relative mRNA levels of six genes related to the long-term memory (LTM) in zebrafish larvae exposed to GQDs at 7 dpf, including grin1a, grin2a, grin3a, gria3a, gria4a, and camk2a. Data between control and each GQDs treatment are analyzed with One-Way ANOVA and the values of * p ≤ 0.05, * * p ≤ 0.01, and * ** p ≤ 0.001 are considered as significant levels.

Immunoanalysis and qPCR quantification for glutamine synthase and olig2 in zebrafish larvae. (a) In situ whole-mount immunofluorescence analysis of Glul expression in zebrafish larvae Tg (olig2: DsRed2) exposed to different concentrations of GQDs at 4.5 dpf. White arrows indicated the expression abundance of Glul in the notochord of zebrafish larvae. (b) The total glutamine levels in different treatments. (c) The mRNA expression levels of glul in zebrafish larvae exposed to different concentrations of GQDs. (d) The mRNA expression levels of olig2 in zebrafish larvae exposed to different concentrations of GQDs. All data are presented as mean ± SEM and One-way ANOVA is employed for the statistical analysis, and the levels of * p ≤ 0.05 and * * p ≤ 0.01 are considered to be significant. (e) Fluorescent detection of the apoptosis in zebrafish larvae Tg (olig2: DsRed2) exposed to different concentrations of GQDs at 7 dpf. Yellow arrows pointed to the apoptotic areas in the forebrain in zebrafish larvae.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) induce thigmotactic effect in zebrafish larvae via modulating key genes and metabolites related to synaptic plasticity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) recently gain much attention for its medicinal values in treating diseases such as neurodegeneration and inflammations. However, owing to the high permeability of GQDs across the blood-brain barrier, whether its retention in the central nervous system (CNS) perturbs neurobehaviors remains less reported. In the study, the locomotion of zebrafish larvae (Danio rerio) was fully evaluated when administrated by two GQDs in a concentration gradient, respectively as reduced-GQDs (R-GQDs): 150, 300, 600, 1200, and 2400 g/L, and graphene oxide QDs (GOQDs): 60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 g/L. After exposure, the larvae were kept for locomotion analysis within one week’s depuration. Substantial data showed that the basal locomotor activity of zebrafish larvae was not significantly changed by both two GQDs at low concentrations while weakened greatly with the increase of concentrations, and the total ATP levels of zebrafish larvae were also found to decrease significantly when exposed to the highest concentrations of GQDs. Next, the thigmotactic effect was observed to be remarkably induced in larvae by both two GQDs at any concentrations during exposure, and remained strong in larvae treated by high concentrations of R-GQDs after 7 days’ depuration. To be noted, we found that GQDs affected the synaptic plasticity via downregulating the mRNA levels of NMDA and AMPA receptor family members as well as the total glutamine levels in zebrafish larvae. Together, our study presented robust data underlying the locomotor abnormalities aroused by GQDs in zebrafish larvae and indicated the potential adverse effects of GQDs on synaptic plasticity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since its first isolation from 2004, two-dimensional graphene (G) stirs up a spree of research interests owing to its outstanding properties including mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and large surface area (Ghaffarkhah et al. 2022). Graphene (G) is composed of sp
2 hybridized carbon atoms linked to one another arranged in a honeycomb lattice, which shows great biomedical potentials as the result of its large surface areas for loading drugs, high near-infrared absorbance for photothermal therapies, surface modification for biosensors, and mechanic flexibility for tissue engineering (Song et al. 2020). G and a series of G derivatives have been developed for biomedical purposes and especially graphene oxide (GO) is highly recommended for its amphiphilicity and the easy manipulation of surface characters (Chung et al. 2013). However, G, GO, and many others have been frequently reported for their intrinsic toxicity arised from their graphene-based surfaces, sizes, number of layers, fabrication methods, administration routes, etc (Fadeel et al. 2018). As the next generation G derivatives, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) draw great attention for its high biocompatibility and more widely biomedical applications over the past few years. GQDs are ultrasmall G nanoparticles with the lateral dimensions usually below 10 nm and develop superior physicochemical properties originated from its quantum confinement effect and edge effect (Yan et al. 2019). GQDs show many advantages including tunable photoluminescence, chemical stability, anti- and pro-oxidant activities, which have been engineered as sensitive fluorophores to monitor living cells and drug carriers to assist target therapies in in vivo systems. More strikingly, GQDs are recently manifested as potent pharmaceutical agents to treat diseases such as inflammations (Lee et al. 2020; Ren et al. 2018; Tosic et al. 2019; Volarevic et al. 2014; Zhang et al. 2021; Zhao et al. 2022), amylosis (Kim et al. 2018; Liu et al. 2015; Wang et al. 2018; Yousaf et al. 2017) and metabolic disorder (Kang et al. 2021), thereby opening up a new door for the medicinal uses of G family members.

The claim of low toxicity of GQDs has been strongly supported by most in vitro studies, but there remain inconsistent findings about the toxicity of GQDs that may arise from its surface modification, including the oxidation, surface functionalization, atomic doping, etc (Henna and Pramod, 2020; Wang et al. 2016). It has been evidenced that hydroxylated GQDs (HO-GQDs) are high toxic to human esophageal epithelial cells (HET-1A) even at a relatively low exposure concentration (25 µg/mL), causing DNA damage, cell cycle arrest, the disruption of microtubule structure and microtubule regrowth, and the suppression of DNA damage repair genes (Li et al. 2018). The toxicity of HO-GQDs were further verified in mice with oral administration of HO-GQDs (5 mg/kg), which caused significant intestinal injuries, such as enhanced intestinal penetrability, shortened villi, and crypt loss (Yu et al. 2019). Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and aminated GQDs (A-GQDs) were once verified as two safety nanoparticles that have been exploited as fluorophores for real-time bioimaging and photodynamic agents to treat cancers and other diseases (Kuo et al. 2017; Kuo et al. 2018; Liu et al. 2019; Magaela et al. 2022). However, recent findings warned the health risks of these two GQDs when used in in vivo therapies. The toxicity of N-GQDs was firstly reported for its perturbation on the classical AhR pathway, and later found in zebrafish embryos with the activation of multiple pathways (e.g. complement system, cytochrome P450 system) and the disruption of the redox-sensitive balance even at a moderate concentration (50 µg/mL) (Deng et al. 2019; Deng et al. 2018). Recently, two interrelated studies revealed that N-GQDs can cause ferroptosis in microglia cells and induce neuroapoptosis via disturbing intracellular calcium equilibrium in mice (Wu et al. 2020; Wu et al. 2022). A-GQDs were firstly reported to induce the apoptosis of alveolar macrophages through inducing DNA cleavage via H-bonding and π-π stacking and showed many similarities with N-GQDs not only in the functional groups, and also in the transcriptomic responses aroused by both GQDs (Deng et al. 2022; Xu et al. 2018). In addition, the global changes of DNA methylation were observed in zebrafish when exposed to different GQDs (Hu et al. 2019), and also found in embryonic stem cells (Ku et al. 2021). Generally, whether the specific adverse effect aroused by one or two types of GQDs is also shared by other sources of GQDs remains less investigated. A latest inspiring study revealed that four types of GQDs including reduced GQDs (R-GQDs), graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), carboxylated GQDs (C-GQDs), and A-GQDs can induce the expression of gene clusters in relation to calcium/potassium ion channels and spliceosomes with varying degrees of regulatory effects (Deng et al. 2022), thereby providing guidelines to explore the latent post-translational and epigenetic effects exerted by GQDs more comprehensively. Until now, it remains less reported whether GQDs exert persistent or transient adverse effects on neurobehaviors despite the neuroapoptosis observed in mice treated with N-GQDs (Wu et al. 2020; Yan et al. 2020). In view of the easy permeability of GQDs across brain-blood barriers (Li et al. 2016; Seven et al. 2021; Zhou et al. 2019), understanding how GQDs affect neurobehaviors through its likely perturbation on the neuron-related molecular markers and neurotransmitters is vital before its legally approval for clinical studies in the future.

Zebrafish has been considered as an ideal animal model for high-throughput drug screening owing to its high fecundity, low-cost husbandry, transparent body, and a suit of conserved protein families that are functionally comparable to that of vertebrates (Postlethwait et al. 1998). The teleost fish shows structural homology with human brain in hypothalamus, optic tracts, olfactory system, spinal cord and cranial nerves, which undergo a rapid development during embryonic stage, thus lending itself to being the preferred vertebrate model for the study of neuronal functions when bearing with external pressures, such as drugs, chemicals, and environmental pollutants (Nishimura et al. 2015; Zhao et al. 2022b). The small size of zebrafish makes it to be easily manipulated and observed under an automated behavior analyzer within a relatively small chamber. Further, zebrafish embryos were found to be more sensitive to exogenous hazards relative to mouse embryonic cells (Inoue et al. 2016). The small fish has been widely applied for the behavioral analysis of a series of carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (Cheng et al. 2007), graphene (Manjunatha et al. 2018), graphene oxide (Chen et al. 2016), carbon quantum dots (Chousidis et al. 2020), etc. As the precursors of GQDs, both G and GO were found to change the locomotor activities of zebrafish to varying degrees upon the exposure concentrations and the lateral sizes (Audira et al. 2021; Cao et al. 2021; Chen et al. 2020). Thus, whether GQDs inherit the nano-bio effect from its precursors remains to be examined especially considering the potential medicinal applications of GQDs in the future.

In the present study, R-GQDs and GOQDs were selected for investigating their effect on the locomotion of zebrafish larvae during the early stage of development. Although these two GQDs were attested to be low toxicity in previous studies (Deng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2018), their threshold doses initiating apoptosis or death remain to be not determined yet. To systemically assess the potential adverse effects of GQDs on locomotor activity and related biochemical changes, we firstly investigated the biodistribution and depuration of GQDs in zebrafish larvae, and assessed the locomotor responses of zebrafish larvae exposed to GQDs in a concentration gradient. Next, the survival rates, reactive oxygen species (ROS), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and a series of genes related to neurobehavioral disorders were examined in zebrafish larvae exposed to different concentrations of GQDs. Our results proved an alert for the medicinal uses of GQDs in treating CNS-related diseases in the future.

In the present study, reduced GQDs (R-GQDs, XF215) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs, XF074) were both purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Nanostructures of GQDs were scanned using a Transmission Electron Microscope (Talos F200X, FEI, USA) under the operating voltage of 200 kV. Infrared spectroscopy analysis was performed with an Agilent Cary 630 FTIR Spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA) to compare the functional commonality of chemical bonds between these two GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra for the two types of GQDs were obtained using PHI-5000 VersaProbe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured by the UV spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan), and the surface charges of the two GQDs are quantified using Zetasizer Nano ZS apparatus (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, U.K.) in ultrapure water (ddH2O), 1 × PBS and DMEM mediums (Gibco).

Two hundred of wild-type AB zebrafish at 4-months old were purchased from China Zebrafish Resource Center (CZRC, Wuhan, China) and maintained at 28 ℃ and a constant photoperiod (LD 14: 10). After acclimation for one week, zebrafish were coupled for oviposition and the laid eggs were collected at 2 h post fertilization and transferred to a 10 cm Petri dish containing 1 × E3 embryonic culture medium (5.0 mM NaCl, 0.44 mM CaCl2, 0.17 mM KCl, 0.33 mM MgSO4; pH 7.4). At 4 h post fertilization (hpf), 50 embryos were slightly pipetted into each well of a six-well plate filled with different concentrations of GQDs in 1 × E3 medium, respectively as R-GQDs (150, 300, 600, 1200, and 2400 g/L) and GOQDs (60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 g/L). The exposure solutions were newly prepared for every day. In total, each concentration of GQDs including the control was performed in four wells. Survival rates were recorded from 2 days post fertilization (dpf) to 7 dpf during the exposure period, and after that zebrafish larvae in each treatment were removed to a cubic container (20 × 20 × 10 cm) for depuration for one week. Next, transgenic zebrafish strains including Tg (fli: EGFP), Tg (Na+/K+-ATPase: EGFP), and Tg (olig2: DsRed2) were employed in the study, all of which were ordered from CZRC and raised under the same condition as described above.

R-GQDs were characteristic of strong red fluorescence emission when stimulated by 488 nm excitation wavelength. The bioaccumulation of R-GQDs was examined using the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, FV1200, Olympus, Japan) in zebrafish strain Tg (fli: EGFP) at 7 dpf. To assess the depuration process of R-GQDs in zebrafish larvae, we used an inverted microscope (Nikon SMZ18, Japan) to investigate the fluorescence emitted from larval fish at the end of the exposure (7 dpf) and the following depuration stage (10 dpf, 14 dpf, and 18 dpf). Next, the microinjection technique was also employed to monitor the depuration of R-GQDs in zebrafish. A stock solution (1 mg/mL) of R-GQDs was prepared using 1 × E3 medium, and after a 30 s ultrasonic treatment (100 W), a total of 4 nL R-GQDs (1 mg/mL) solution was injected into the heart region of the larvae at 6 dpf using a microinjector (Eppendorf, Germany), after which the fish were observed at 4 day, 8 day, and 12 day post microinjection under the inverted microscope (Nikon SMZ18, Japan). To avoid the autofluoresence emitted from the pigmentation of zebrafish larvae, the fish used in the observation of depuration process were depigmented by 20 μM 1-Phenyl-2-thiourea (PTU) from 4 hpf.

The locomotor activity of zebrafish is controlled by light, and larvae show typical behavioral responses triggered by the dark-light switch. The larvae at 7 dpf were tracked individually on a 24-well plate (one larvae per well) using the DanioVision system and EthoVision XT software (v.15.0, Noldus). After 10 min of habituation in the dark, the larvae were exposed to alternating 10-min light and dark periods for a total of 50 min. After exposure, zebrafish larvae at 10 dpf and 14 dpf during depuration period were further investigated for their locomotor activity using the same parametric setting. During exposure and depuration period, 20–24 zebrafish larvae were randomly chosen from each treatment for locomotion analysis.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels were detected in zebrafish larvae using 2′, 7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA, Sigma) according to a previous study (Kosmehl et al. 2006) and the extraction produre was a little improved. Briefly, 30 larvae from each well were collected into a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube from each treatment and anesthetized using newly-prepared 1 × E3 medium containing tricaine methanesulfonate (40 mg/L). After washing with water for two times, the fish were quickly sacrificed on ice and ground with a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube containing 1 mL 0.05% (w/v) trypsin/EDTA solution (Gibco) and incubated for 5 min. After filtered with 70 µm cell strainers, the homogenate was centrifuged at 1, 500g for 8 min at 4 °C. The cell pellet was slightly collected in a new 1.5 centrifuge tube and resuspended using 400 μL 0.9% physiological saline, and 200 μL cell suspension was removed to a 96 cell plate and incubated with 10 M DCFH-DA for 20 min. Four biological replicates were designed for control and GQDs treatments at different concentrations. The fluorescent intensity was read on Infinite M200 Pro (Tecan, Switzerland) and finally determined according to the cell numbers counted from an autocounter. With the same sampling strategy used in ROS detection, the total ATP content of zebrafish larvae was assayed at 7 dpf according to the previous study using a chemiluminescence ATP determination kit (Beyotime, China, Cat: S0027) (Deng et al. 2022). Next, the total glutamine content of zebrafish larvae in different treatments was detected at 7 dpf with a glutamine assay kit (A074–1–1) provided by Nanjing Jiancheng Bio-engineering Institute (Nanjing, Jiangsu, China).

Zebrafish strain Tg (olig2: DsRed2) exposed to GQDs from low to medium to high levels at 4.5 dpf were used to detect the expression levels of glutamine synthetase (Glul). Immunofluorescent analysis was performed according to the previous study (Sorrells et al. 2013). Briefly, 15 embryos at 4.5 dpf from each treatment were removed to a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube and washed with 1 mL 1 × PBS for 2 times. After the removal of 1 × PBS, each tube was added into 1 mL of 4% PFA and placed on a gentle rocker at 4 ℃ overnight. On the following day the samples were treated by ice-cold 100% methanol and 1 × PDT, and then incubated with rabbit anti-activated Glul antibody (GTX109121, Genetex Inc., Irvine, CA, USA) at 4 ℃ overnight. After the removal of the primary antibody solutions, the samples were washed with 1 × PDT and incubated with anti-rabbit FITC-conjugated secondary antibody (A0562, Beyotime, Beijing, China) for 2 h at room temperature. After that, embryos were washed with 1 × PDT and maintained in a freshly 1 × PDT solution and observed under the inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon SMZ18, Japan). Immunochemical analysis was performed with zebrafish larvae at 7 dpf, and 3 larvae from each treatment were cut with the freezing microtome (LS-2900, Shenyang Longshou Electronic Instrument Co., Ltd.) and serial slices of with an average thickness of 2–4 µm were prepared for DAPI dying and fluorescent scanning (Pannoramic 250, 3DHISTECH, Hungary).

Statistical analysis was performed with SPSS22.0 software (IBM, USA), and the significant levels between control and different treatments were determined by One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) following by Dunnett's t test. A p value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

TEM scanning displayed the range of the nanoscale (2–4 nm) and lattice spacing (0.24–0.25 nm) of R-GQDs and GOQDs ( Fig. 1a-c), in accordance with the previous reports (Deng et al. 2022; Li et al. 2019). The absorption peaks at about 1743, 1563, 1331, and 1098 cm−1 reveal the existence of CO, CC, C-N, and C-O in both two GQDs (Fig. 1d). Separately, R-GQDs showed characteristic peaks including N-H at 3130 cm−1, alkyl C-H stretching vibration at 2985 and 2795 cm−1, and aromatic C-H at 1269 and 1193 cm−1, while GOQDs have broad absorption band from 3217 to 3374 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of -OH. The XPS survey spectra of both two GQDs showed obvious peaks of C1s at about 284.3 eV and O1s at about 532.4 eV, and R-GQDs are identified to contain high content of N, far more than that in GOQDs (Fig. 1e), and the ratio of amino group in R-GQDs was 1.35%, which was more than two times than that of GOQDs (Fig. 1f). The high resolution XPS of O spectra revealed that besides CO and C-O-C, GOQDs have the hydroxyl groups, in accordant with the FTIR spectra. In the C 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra, R-GQDs have the equal ratio of C-O and OC-O, which means O existed mainly in the form of carboxyl group. Meanwhile, the table exhibited the element distribution of different GQDs from the XPS results (Fig. 1g), and from the table the high ratio of oxygen in GOQD was identified. Fig. 1h demonstrated that the extremely high values of zeta potential of both two GQDs in ultrapure water (pH 7.0), and decreased greatly in both 1 ×PBS and DMEM mediums. The UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence emission spectra of the two GQDs were acquired from the aqueous solution under UV–vis radiation, and the luminescence was observed for the two GQDs as the increasing excitation from 450 nm to 550 nm (R-GQDs) and from 324 nm to 475 nm (GOQDs), respectively (Supplementary Fig. S1). The emission spectra of the two GQDs were characterized with the strongest emission at around 600 nm (R-GQDs) and 570 nm (GOQDs) under the excitation of 525 nm and 375 nm, which resulted in different fluorescent colors, i.e. purple fluorescence for R-GQDs and light yellow fluorescence for GOQDs.

After 7 days of successive exposure, we observed that R-GQDs accumulated in the forebrain of zebrafish larvae Tg (fli: EGFP) ( Fig. 2a), meanwhile, intensive red fluorescence emitted from GQDs was found in the heart, yolk sac, pronephric region and gastrointestinal system of zebrafish larvae, as indicated by the yellow arrows. After exposure, the red fluorescence emitted by R-GQDs was still found in the nephric and intestinal system of zebrafish larvae from 10 dpf to 14 dpf, and disappeared until 18 dpf (Fig. 2b). When larvae at 6 dpf were injected by 4 nL R-GQDs solution (1 mg/mL), the red fluorescence was maintained in the kidney, yolk sac, and intestine at 10 dpf (equal to 4 days post microinjection), and attenuated 14 dpf (equal to 8 days post microinjection), and died out at 18 dpf (Fig. 2c). The decay of the fluorescence in the body was accompanied with the fecal excreted from the fish, as shown in Fig. 2d, and these two different treatments (i.e. waterborne exposure and microinjection) indicated the similar metabolic rate of R-GQDs in larvae.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs altered the basal locomotor activity of zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the above analysis of bioaccumulation and depuration of GQDs in zebrafish larvae, locomotion analysis was performed during exposure (7 dpf) and depuration period (10 dpf and 14 dpf) (
Fig. 3a-b). According to the data from the developmental toxicity assay (Supplemental Fig. 2), a series of sublethal stepwise concentration gradients for these two GQDs were designed, respectively as R-GQDs (150, 300, 600, 1200, and 2400 g/L) and GOQDs (60, 120, 240, 480, and 960 g/L). After 7 days of successive exposure, the basal locomotor activities of larvae including distance moved and the average velocity were not significantly by both two GQDs at low concentrations while hypoactive when concentrations increased (Fig. 3c-d). After exposure, the larvae treated by these two GQDs at 10 dpf underwent a rebound effect of locomotor activity, and recovered to the state that was comparable to that of the control at 14 dpf. To ascertain the effect of light-dark transition on the locomotion, the data of distance moved during 10-min light and dark periods were extracted from total distance moved and showed that the hypoactivity induced by GQDs was basically not affected by the light-dark switch ( Fig. 4a-b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs induced thigmotaxis of zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Thigmotaxis, also named as wall-hugging, is the propensity to avoid the center of an arena and stay or move in close proximity to the boundaries of the environment, and this term is often used as a validated indicator of anxiety and depression in zebrafish (Blaser et al. 2010). In the study, thigmotaxis of larvae was determined by its swimming frequency in the outer zone ( Fig. 5a), and the cumulative duration (%), an index meant the frequency of the time that the fish spent in the inner zone or the outer zone displayed that larvae administrated by R-GQDs and GOQDs at all concentrations spent more time to wander in the outer space than did they in the inner space at 7 dpf (Fig. 5b-c). At 10 dpf during depuration, the thigmotactic effect remained stronger in R-GQDs treated groups when compared to that in control, and attenuated to varying extent upon the concentrations in GOQDs treatment. At 14 dpf during depuration, the thigmotactic effects attenuated greatly in GOQDs treatments, but enhanced significantly in larvae treated by the lowest concentration (150 g/L) of R-GQDs. As specifically shown in the heatmaps, the thigmotactic effect was basically triggered by light stimulus in R-GQDs treated larvae, but independent of the light-dark transition in GOQDs treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs did not induce oxidative stress but inhibited the production of ATP
ce:section.ce:para: With the increase of the exposure concentrations of both two GQDs, we found that ROS levels remained not significantly changed, while slightly decreased in larvae treated by R-GQDs at 600 g/L and GOQDs at 960 g/L, but not to significant levels ( Fig. 6a). Owing to the muscle contraction and nerve impulse propagation supported by sustained energy sources supply such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP), and we found that the total ATP levels decreased significantly in zebrafish larvae exposed to the highest concentrations of GQDs at 7dpf, while greatly decreased at the highest concentration in contrast to that in control larvae (Fig. 6b). Further, we examined the expression levels of one typical ion channel type that is closely related to ATP production. The transgenic zebrafish strain Tg (Na+/K+-ATPase: EGFP), which specifically expresses Na, K-ATPase alpha1A4 (Atp1a1a.4) in its pronephric region. With the increase of exposure concentrations of GQDs, the green fluorescence expression in the transgenic fish attenuated greatly, as shown in Fig. 6c, more than half of the tested fish (12/15, 13/15) were characterized with the diminished fluorescence when treated by 2400 g/L R-GQDs and 960 g/L GOQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs suppressed the mRNA levels of key genes related to synaptic plasticity
ce:section.ce:para: It was noticeable that the thigmotactic effect was triggered by R-GQDs and GOQDs at the lowest concentration (150 g/L and 60 g/L, respectively, Fig. 5). Therefore, we concluded that the thigmotaxis could be induced by these two GQDs at 100 g/L, and then we retrospected our recently released RNA-seq data that were acquired from zebrafish larvae exposed to four GQDs at the same exposure concentration (100 g/L), including R-GQDs, GOQDs, carboxylated GQDs (C-GQDs), and aminated GQDs (A-GQDs) (Deng et al. 2022), and found that a series of genes closely related to synaptic plasticity were significantly regulated by both two GQDs. As shown in Supplemental Fig. 3, almost all genes belonging to N-methyl-D-aspartic acid receptor (NMDA) family and α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole-propionicacid receptor (AMPA) family were transcriptionally inhibited by both two GQDs at 100 g/L. To ascertain whether the transcriptomic perturbation was aroused by R-GQDs and GQDs in the study, we performed qPCR with six genes (grin1a, grin2a, grin3a, gria3a, gria4a, and camk2a) in typically relation to synaptic plasticity for zebrafish larvae treated by R-GQDs and GOQDs from low to medium to high concentrations, respectively R-GQDs (150–600–2400 g/L) and GOQDs (60–240–960 g/L). Results showed that the mRNA levels of the selected NMDA and AMPA receptors were congruently downregulated by both two GQDs, and to significant levels when the concentrations increased ( Fig. 7). In addition, calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase IIα (CaMKII), a key molecular substrate responsible for synaptic plasticity and memory, was identified to be remarkably transcriptionally suppressed by R-GQDs from low-to-high concentrations and by GOQDs from medium-to-high concentrations.

Glutamate as a widely-distributed excitory neurotransmitter acts key roles in modulating the synaptic plasticity along with NMDA and AMPA receptor families (Lovinger, 2010). Glutamate is synthesized by the enzyme glutamine synthetase (Glul) from glutamate and ammonia (glutamate + ATP + NH3 → glutamine + ADP + phosphate), thus we made a further investigation about the expression of glutamine synthetase (Glul) and associated changes of the total glutamine levels in zebrafish larvae exposed to R-GQDs and GOQDs. Firstly, using the transgenic zebrafish strain Tg (olig2: DsRed2), we found that more than half of the larvae (R-GQDs: 10/14, GOQDs: 8/13) displayed diminished expression levels of the fluorescent protein (Glul) and Olig2 in the notochord when exposed to the highest concentrations of R-GQDs (2400 g/L) and GOQDs (960 g/L) at 4.5 dpf ( Fig. 8a), while GOQDs treated larvae (9/12) at the lowest concentration (60 g/L) displayed a slight enhancement of Glul and Olig2 expression when compared to control. The detected total glutamine content showed the similar changes to the transcriptional changes of glul in zebrafish larvae exposed to different concentrations of GQDs, and to significant levels at the highest concentrations in contrast to that in control treatment (Fig. 8b). Correlatively, qPCR data showed that the significantly decreased mRNA levels of glul in zebrafish larvae treated by R-GQDs at 2400 g/L and GOQDs at 240 and 960 g/L when compared to control group, and no significant changes of glul mRNA levels were observed in groups treated by low-to-medium concentrations of R-GQDs (Fig. 8c). As a typical neural marker for oligodendrocyte progenitor cells, the declined fluorescent expression levels of oligodendrocyte lineage transcription factor 2 (Olig2) were found in zebrafish larvae Tg (olig2: DsRed2) when exposed the high concentrations of GQDs at 4 dpf, we also identified that olig2 was significantly inhibited by GQDs from medium-to-high concentrations (Fig. 8d). We also used DAPI to detect the apoptosis with an emphasis on the brain of zebrafish larvae (olig2: DsRed2) exposed to low-to-high GQDs, and found that low concentrations of both two GQDs did not arouse apoptosis, while apoptotic cells occurred in the brain of larvae exposed to the medium concentrations of GQDs and increased at the highest concentrations (Fig. 8e).

In the study, the observation of R-GQDs accumulated in brain of zebrafish larvae was basically in agreement with previous studies (Li et al. 2016; Zhou et al. 2019), which demonstrated the penetration of carbon quantum dots derived from carbon power and citric acid across the brain-blood barrier in zebrafish embryos. Also, the biodistribution of R-GQDs in different organs of zebrafish larvae was basically accordant with that of GOQDs reported in a previous study (Ren et al. 2018). The two different treatments including waterborne exposure and microinjection of GQDs showed the similar metabolic process of GQDs in zebrafish larvae, suggesting that the uptake and metabolism of GQDs are independent of the exposure assays.

R-GQDs and GOQDs have been evidenced as two relatively safety nanoparticles in previous studies (Deng et al. 2022; Li et al. 2019), and our present study confirmed the high tolerance of zebrafish larvae to these two GQDs. ROS have been identified as the harmful factor that leads to apoptosis when carbon-based nanomaterials were used in in vitro or in vivo systems, such as carbon nanotubes, G, and GO (Jia et al. 2019; Pulskamp et al. 2007). A previous study had revealed that R-GQDs and GOQDs at 100 g/L did not induce significant oxidative stress in HL-7702 cells (Li et al. 2019), and GOQDs had also been reported for its strong intrinsic peroxidase-like ability to scavenge ROS (Ren et al. 2018). Likewise, our study showed that ROS levels in zebrafish larvae were not significantly changed even at the high concentrations of R-GQDs and GOQDs. With the increase of the exposure concentrations of GQDs, the hypoactivity was aroused in zebrafish larvae. The finding of the reduction of ATP levels in zebrafish larvae exposed to high concentrations of GQDs demonstrated that the energy homeostasis was strongly disturbed, suggesting that the decreased locomotor activity. In the previous study, we found that four different GQDs (i.e. R-GQDs, GOQDs, C-GQDs and A-GQDs) disrupted the mRNA levels of potassium/calcium (K+/Ca2+) channel genes, which act as voltage-channels to mediate ion influx underlying electrical transmission of neural signals and the muscular responses (Amberg et al. 2003; Bannister and Beam, 2013; Lai and Jan, 2006). Thus, together with the low fluorescent expression levels of Atp1a1a.4, one Na, K-ATPase subtype specifically expressed in the pronephric region, our data showed the negatively regulation of K+/Ca2+ by GQDs may cause the greatly decrease of ATP levels, and then affected the muscle contraction and ensuing swimming activity of zebrafish larvae.

Based on a deep analysis of RNA-seq data released from our previous study (Deng et al. 2022), the current study employed qPCR to further confirm the suppressing effects of GQDs on the mRNA expression levels of NMDA and AMPA receptor family members, suggesting that GQDs could potentiate the long-term depression (LTD), an important form of synaptic plasticity. Meanwhile, these glutamate receptors are often interacted with voltage-gated calcium channels to mediate the ion current that activates or de-activates the neurons (Diering and Huganir, 2018). As a typical marker of the synaptic plasticity, CaMKII was found to be inhibited to varying extent in its protein levels in zebrafish larvae exposed to 100 g/L R-GQDs and GOQDs at 7 dpf in the previous study (Deng et al. 2022), and also the mRNA levels of camk2a were identified to be notably inhibited by both two GQDs with the increase of the exposure concentrations in the present study. Except the molecular basis about the disruption of synaptic plasticity aroused by GQDs, we found that the total glutamine levels along with the transcriptional levels of glutamine synthetase (glul) decreased significantly in zebrafish exposed to high concentrations of GQDs at 7 dpf, implying that the total glutamate levels were negatively regulated by GQDs, to varying extent upon the concentrations. As the natural endogenous ligand of NMDA and AMPA receptors, glutamate acts an excitatory neurotransmitter to initiate the synaptic transmission through binding with endogenous receptors and involve in the cognitive functions such as learning and memory (Barnes et al. 2020). Therefore, our data about the disturbing effects of GQDs on synaptic plasticity underlay the thigmotactic effects induced by GQDs, suggesting that zebrafish larvae exposed to GQDs may suffer from depression or anxiety-like behaviors. In addition, OLIG2 as an oligodendrocyte marker was identified to be transcriptionally downregulated by GQDs, suggesting that high concentrations of GQDs may arrest the early differentiation of oligodendrocyte progenitor cells (OPC), which have been reported as the only glial cells underlying the direct synapses with neurons and play key roles in synaptic plasticity (Bergles and Richardson, 2015). Since GQDs have been reported it medicinal potential for treating Parkinson’s’ disease (Kim et al. 2018), neuroinflammation (Tosic et al. 2019), and neurotoxicity (Ren et al. 2018), its potential risk on neurobehaviors should not be overlooked, and more comprehensive studies would be required for assessing the toxicity mechanisms underlying the neurobehavioral disorders induced by GQDs.

The current study showed the bioaccumulation of GQDs in brain, heart, kidney, yolk sac, and gastrointestinal system of zebrafish larvae. The depuration process of GQDs was not affected by different treatments (waterborne exposure versus microinjection), and kidney was the main organ responsible for GQDs metabolism. The basal locomotor activities including distance moved and velocity decreased to varying extent by R-GQDs and GOQDs with the elevation of exposure concentrations, while strong thigmotactic effect was observed in larvae treated by these two GQDs even at the lowest concentrations. Locomotor disorders induced by GQDs during exposure attenuated much in zebrafish after the stop of exposure. Along with the dystropic activities, we found that both R-GQDs and GOQDs could significantly perturb the transcriptional expression levels of synaptic plasticity-related genes as well as the total glutamine levels in zebrafish larvae. Our study revealed the locomotion abnormalities and associated molecular mechanisms underlying the long-term depression potentiation induced by GQDs, thereby suggesting that GQDs may exert adverse effects on the memory and learning even at relatively low concentrations. Thus, more comprehensively assessments would be required before the medicinal uses of GQDs for treating diseases especially for CNS-related disorders.

Animal experiments were performed according to the ‘Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals’ (Eighth Edition, 2011. ILARCLS, National Research Council, Washington, D.C.). The animal protocol was approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing in China and by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (Approval ID: ZKCQY0914).
